body_txt: Fauns and Satyrs, while originally quite different, have often been fused together , both in the original myths and modern fiction, which is why they share a page. Both are human from the waist up, but fauns have the legs of deer, while satyrs have the legs of goats. Both may have pointed ears , or horns , or both — and for some reason, both are very likely to be male (though fauns seem a bit more likely to have females.) Originally, satyrs had human legs and the tail and ears of either horses or donkeys, although by the classical Greek period they had lost the legs and mostly resembled humans with exaggerated ugly features and genitalia . In earlier Greek art they were portrayed as quite ugly, although later they became more youthful and graceful. They were associated with sex , wine , and pipe playing (the Classical Greek equivalent of Sex, Drugs, and Rock & Roll ). They also laughed at everything, and had reverence for none but their patron deity, Dionysus, the god of wine and madness, with whom they cavorted in wild places. Originally distinct from them was Pan, the goat-legged and -horned god of shepherds, music, woodlands, and wild places. Distinct still were fauns, who originated in Italic culture as a type of nature and forest deity that served the god Faunus. They were tutelary deities of the wilderness, and lacked the association with debauchery and violence that Greek satyrs had. As the Greek and Roman religions syncretized, these types of beings came to be conflated with one another due to being male broadly animal-featured humanoids associated with wild places, glens, and hills; the resulting "hybrids", still called Faunī in Latin and Satyroi in Greek, retained the goatish features of Pan, the wild character of satyrs, and the love of the wilderness of all of these beings. This was also the type to be most commonly used in Renaissance art after a revival of interest in Classical material, where they commonly depicted cavorting with nymphs and centaurs in forest glens, and is thusly the most common in modern fiction. In modern fiction, satyrs and/or fauns (whether they use one name or both or whether they're separate beings can vary case-by-case) are usually depicted as cheerful hedonists who live in wild places and live lives focused on music, wine, and trysts; depending on the work, these can range from their being jovial bon vivants to debased lechers. They're usually associated with the Greek deities and Greek-inspired settings, fairies , or both; in particular, they're very frequent in the company of nymphs and dryads —their usual female counterparts and amorous targets— and of centaurs . They're not usually depicted as the types for civilized or settled life, instead usually being found rollicking in the glens and fields of Arcadia , the Ghibli Hills , and the Enchanted Forest . The most famous of all goat people is the great god Pan , and his Roman counterpart Faunus/Inuus. Pan was the god of all wild creatures, and had a trickster streak a mile wide . Despite his wildness and temper (his shouts would inspire pan -ic in all who heard him) he was a true friend to shepherds and little critters . And if your Satyr lives underground in a cavern full of flame, well, he might be a bit nastier... See Our Centaurs Are Different for a similar half-man half-beast creature from Classical Mythology .
